mindMap.mermaidCode:
mindmap
  root((ce 323: Mass Storage Systems))
    HDD Fundamentals and Performance
      Importance & Relevance
        Historical Trend: Importance decreased due to SSDs
        Resurgence: Critical for Data Centers (especially with AI growth)
        Still used in Laptops (some) and for High-Capacity Storage
      Physical Structure
        Platters (Spinal Platters)
          Magnetically-coated material
          Rotate 60-250 times/second
          Common Sizes: 3.5", 2.5", 1.8"
        Arm Assembly
          Monolithic structure
          Magnetic Read-Write Heads
            Do not physically contact platters (prevents Head Crash)
            Multiple heads move together
        Cylinders
          Equidistant/Equicentric tracks on all platters
          Minimizes arm movement (read/write as much as possible per cylinder)
          Storage sequence by filling one cylinder then moving to next
      Performance Characteristics
        Transfer Rate
          Rate data flows between drive and computer
          Head read speed must match transfer rate (to avoid buffering delays)
          Theoretical: ~6 Gb/sec
          Effective (real): ~1 Gb/sec
        Access Time (Random-Access Time)
          Components: Positional Delay + Rotational Delay + Transfer Time + Controller Overhead
          Positional Delay (Seek Time)
            Time to move arm assembly to desired cylinder
            Dominant factor affecting I/O time
            Range: 3ms to 12ms (9ms common for desktop)
          Rotational Delay (Rotational Latency)
            Time for desired sector to rotate under the head
            Average Latency = 1 / (2 * RPM / 60)
          Transfer Time
            Time to read/write the data block
        Disk Blocks / Sectors
          Smallest addressable/transferable unit
          Block size typically equals sector size
        Capacities: 30GB to 3TB per drive
    Solid State Drives and Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)
      Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)
        General category for non-volatile storage
        Includes SSDs, USB drives, DRAM disk replacements, smartphone storage
      Solid State Drives (SSDs)
        Characteristics
          No moving parts (no seek time or rotational latency)
          Much faster than HDDs
          More reliable (mechanically)
        Challenges / Disadvantages
          More expensive per MB
          Shorter lifespan due to limited erase cycles (~100,000)
          Lifespan measured in Drive Writes Per Day (DWPD)
          Less raw capacity (historically)
        Operation
          Read/written in "page" increments
          Erased in larger "block" increments
          Cannot overwrite in place (must erase first)
        Performance
          I/O nature changes drastically from HDDs
          Busses can be a bottleneck (connect directly to PCI for NVMe)
      Related Management Concepts (for NVM/SSDs)
        Error Detection and Correction
        Storage Device Management
        Swap-Space Management
        Storage Attachment
    Storage System Management & Scheduling
      Disk Scheduling
        Purpose: Decide "whose I/O request goes next"
        Goal: Optimize disk access (minimize seek time, maximize bandwidth)
        Types: HDD Scheduling, NVM Scheduling
        Operating System Role: OS maintains queue of I/O requests
      Storage Device Management
        General management of storage devices
        Includes Error Detection and Correction
      Swap-Space Management
        Uses disk space as an extension of RAM when physical memory is limited
        Part of mass storage management
      Storage Attachment
        Interfaces for connecting storage to the machine
        e.g., SATA, eSATA, SAS, USB, Fibre Channel (FC), NVMe (PCI direct)
      RAID Structure
        Redundant Array of Independent Disks
        Combines multiple physical drives for performance, reliability, or both
